title: Neobartsia integrifolia
intro: Neobartsia integrifolia, also known as Integrifolia or Chilean rose, is a plant species within the Orobanchaceae family, a group of flowering plants commonly referred to as broomrapes. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, such as inflammation, fever, and respiratory problems, due to its potential anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and antimicrobial properties. Bioactive constituents of Neobartsia integrifolia include iridoid glycosides, alkaloids, and phenolic compounds, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made from Neobartsia integrifolia include teas, infusions, and tinctures, often used to alleviate symptoms of colds, coughs, and other respiratory infections.
taxonomy: Neobartsia integrifolia is a species of flowering plant in the family Orobanchaceae. It is native to North America and is commonly found in the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. The classification of Neobartsia integrifolia includes its kingdom as Plantae and its order as Lamiales. It belongs to the genus Neobartsia and has been classified as a type of broomrape plant due to its unique characteristics. Further classification includes it being a member of the class Magnoliopsida and the phylum Angiospermophyta.
